name: Mildred Bailey
abstract: Mildred Bailey (born Mildred Rinker; February 27, 1907 – December 12, 1951) was an American jazz singer during the 1930s and 1940s, known as "The Queen of Swing", "The Rockin' Chair Lady", and "Mrs. Swing".

concerts.label: Mildred Bailey with the Freddie Rich Orchestra

works.t: Cotton Club Parade: I’ve Got the World on a String

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Salute to Fats Waller

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Palmer
about_quotes.evidence: I went there and it was Red Norvo and Mildred Bailey. And I sat in and first thing you know, I didn't know it was being recorded. We were making a record.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Palmer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 24, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I was doing quite a bit of recording with Mildred Bailey. She kept -- she always wanted Roy, Teddy Wilson and myself with her.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: we had done the Mildred Bailey show in the radio days, you know, with the trio.

about_quotes.speaker: Helen Humes
about_quotes.evidence: People have said I sound like Billie Holiday, I sound like Mildred Bailey... And Stan made the point that Mildred (Bailey) had never made a record at that point... So it's not impossible that some people have a little bit of the same kind of feel as another person

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: When he got the job he'd stand in front of the band, and usually that was when Mildred Bailey would work, he'd -- and he'd do his bit with the xylophone, and I would sit down and play saxophone in the section.

about_quotes.speaker: Jay McShann
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] That would be -- I mean, wasn't Jack Teagarden and Mildred Bailey, they were both supposed to have been part Indian, weren't they? [INTERVIEWEE] That's true, yes, yes. That's true.
